metadata.title: Guideline on the investigation of subgroups in confirmatory clinical trials
metadata.issuing_body: Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use (CHMP)

rules.rule_text: A marketing authorisation shall be refused if the risk-benefit balance is not considered to be favourable, or if therapeutic efficacy is insufficiently substantiated by the applicant, or if its qualitative and quantitative composition is not as declared.
rules.source_section: Section 1 - Introduction and problem statement
rules.verbatim_quote: a marketing authorisation shall be refused if, after verification of the particulars and documents listed in Articles 8, 10, 10a, 10b and 10c, it is clear that: (a) the risk-benefit balance is not considered to be favourable; or (b) its therapeutic efficacy is insufficiently substantiated by the applicant; or (c) its qualitative and quantitative composition is not as declared.

rules.rule_text: For confirmatory trials, robust evidence for therapeutic efficacy is required in a relatively broad patient population that is representative of the patient population to be described in Section 4.1 of the SmPC (external validity).
rules.source_section: Section 1 - Introduction and problem statement
rules.verbatim_quote: For confirmatory trials, usually robust evidence for therapeutic efficacy is required in a relatively broad patient population that is representative of patient population to be described in Section 4.1 of the SmPC (external validity).
cross_references: Guideline on Summary of Product Characteristics, Revision 2, September 2009

rules.rule_text: Evidence is considered to be more robust if treatment effects across trials in the application, as well as in relevant subgroups within one trial (internal consistency), are consistent and substantiate the claim to be made for the experimental treatment.
rules.source_section: Section 1 - Introduction and problem statement
rules.verbatim_quote: Evidence is considered to be more robust if treatment effects across the trials in the application, as well as in relevant subgroups within one trial (internal consistency), are consistent and substantiate the claim to be made for the experimental treatment.
cross_references: CPMP/EWP/908/99 - Points to consider on multiplicity issues in clinical trials

rules.rule_text: Analyses of important subgroups (such as gender, age, region, severity of disease, ethnic origin, renal impairment, or differences in absorption or metabolism) should be a regular part of the evaluation of a clinical study.
rules.source_section: Section 1 - Introduction and problem statement
rules.verbatim_quote: analyses of these important subgroups should be a regular part of the evaluation of a clinical study
cross_references: CPMP/EWP/908/99 Points to consider on multiplicity issues in clinical trials

rules.rule_text: It is not acceptable to assume consistent effects across important subgroups without further investigation or discussion.
rules.source_section: Section 4.4 - Problems with conducting multiple subgroup analyses
rules.verbatim_quote: It is not acceptable to assume consistent effects across important subgroups without further investigation or discussion.

rules.rule_text: Post-baseline covariates may be affected by treatment received and will not usually be appropriate to define subgroups for the investigation of a treatment effect.
rules.source_section: Section 4.1 - Defining subgroups
rules.verbatim_quote: Post-baseline covariates may be affected by treatment received and will not usually be appropriate to define subgroups for the investigation of a treatment effect.
cross_references: CHMP/EMA/295050/2013 - Points to consider draft guideline on adjustment for baseline covariates

rules.rule_text: Subgroup definitions should reflect meaningful entities (subpopulations) of the patient population with relevance for decision making in clinical practice. Investigation of (ordered) categorical and continuous factors requires careful consideration when pooling across multiple levels of a single factor or using cut-off points for categorisation of a continuous factor.
rules.source_section: Section 4.1 - Defining subgroups
rules.verbatim_quote: should reflect meaningful entities (subpopulations) of the patient population with relevance for decision making in clinical practice. In consequence, investigation of (ordered) categorical and continuous factors requires careful consideration when pooling across multiple levels of a single factor (e.g. centre or region), or cut-off points for categorisation of a continuous factor are needed.
rules.exceptions: Risk scores based on multiple well-established clinical and demographic factors can be useful for subgroup definition if scores are well established in clinical practice.

rules.rule_text: Particular diligence is needed when considering defining subgroups based on biomarkers and genetic testing, because an almost infinite number of subgroups can be defined based on the combination and categorisation of multiple biomarkers, and knowledge about the prognostic value of the biomarker and its clinical utility is usually lacking.
rules.source_section: Section 4.1 - Defining subgroups
rules.verbatim_quote: Particular diligence is needed therefore when considering defining subgroups in this manner.

rules.rule_text: An indication statement should describe a target population where it has been concluded that there is therapeutic efficacy and a positive risk-benefit. Sub-populations where evidence of efficacy is inadequate, or where risk-benefit is not concluded to be positive, should not be included.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2 - The importance of subgroup analyses in decision making
rules.verbatim_quote: An indication statement should describe a target population where it has been concluded that there is therapeutic efficacy and a positive risk-benefit. Sub-populations where evidence of efficacy is inadequate, or where risk-benefit is not concluded to be positive, should not be included.

rules.rule_text: A well-defined subgroup with a considerably smaller treatment effect will require a separate assessment of efficacy and risk-benefit in order to justify treatment.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2 - The importance of subgroup analyses in decision making
rules.verbatim_quote: A well-defined subgroup with a (true) considerably smaller treatment effect will require a separate assessment of efficacy and risk-benefit in order to justify treatment.

rules.rule_text: Interaction tests used for signal generation in subgroup analyses should employ nominal significance levels greater than 5% because of their role in signal generation and their limited power.
rules.source_section: Section 4.5 - Methods for assessment of the consistency of the treatment effect in subgroups and associated data presentations
rules.verbatim_quote: Because of their role in signal generation, if these tests are conducted nominal significance levels greater than 5% should be employed.

rules.rule_text: Interaction tests cannot be the only tool to identify or exclude inconsistent findings in subgroups that may be of relevance for clinical decision making. The sole reporting of an isolated p-value from a test for interaction is an inadequate basis for decision making.
rules.source_section: Section 4.5 - Methods for assessment of the consistency of the treatment effect in subgroups and associated data presentations
rules.verbatim_quote: Interaction tests cannot be the only tool to identify or exclude inconsistent findings in subgroups that may be of relevance for clinical decision making... the sole reporting of an isolated p-value from a test for interaction is an inadequate basis for decision making.

rules.rule_text: Presentation of subgroup analysis results should include estimates and confidence intervals. Whenever a subgroup analysis is displayed, the analysis of the complement subgroup should also be displayed. Where dichotomous or categorical variables are used to define subgroups, results should be presented in Forest plots.
rules.source_section: Section 4.5 - Methods for assessment of the consistency of the treatment effect in subgroups and associated data presentations
rules.verbatim_quote: Presentation of results should include estimates and confidence intervals. Whenever a subgroup analysis is displayed, the analysis of the complement subgroup should also be displayed. Where dichotomous or categorical variables are used to define subgroups it would be expected to see results presented in Forest plots.
rules.exceptions: For continuous variables, plots should be presented to characterise how the estimated effect of treatment changes over the range of the factor.

rules.rule_text: Subgroup findings that are inconsistent with the overall outcome of the trial should first be explored with statistical methods that investigate whether proper adjustment for covariate imbalances can explain the differences between the overall and subgroup estimate for the treatment effect.
rules.source_section: Section 4.4 - Problems with conducting multiple subgroup analyses
rules.verbatim_quote: It is expected that subgroup findings that are inconsistent with the overall outcome of the trial will first be explored with statistical methods that investigate whether proper adjustment for covariate imbalances can explain the differences between the overall- and the subgroup estimate for the treatment effect.

rules.rule_text: Multiplicity adjustment for subgroup analyses and interaction tests is not recommended. Instead, compensation should be achieved through careful assessment of signals regarding their plausibility and ability to find replication from within or outside the current application.
rules.source_section: Section 4.5 - Methods for assessment of the consistency of the treatment effect in subgroups and associated data presentations
rules.verbatim_quote: adjustment would be counter-intuitive and is not recommended. The fact that multiple subgroups are examined to avoid that untoward effects of treatment are overlooked requires compensation by careful assessment of signals regarding their plausibility and the ability to find replication from within or outside the current application.

rules.rule_text: The credibility of a subgroup finding must be re-evaluated based on the data generated in the clinical trials supporting the MAA, and other external data or knowledge that has emerged during the course of the trial. Biological plausibility and the ability to find replication are the key elements to evaluate credibility.
rules.source_section: Section 4.6 - Credibility of a subgroup finding
rules.verbatim_quote: biological plausibility and the ability to find replication are key elements to evaluate the credibility of a subgroup finding... the credibility of findings in a subgroup of interest needs to be re-evaluated based on the data generated in the clinical trials supporting the MAA, and other external data or knowledge that has emerged during the course of the trial.

rules.rule_text: Having two or more relevant sources of evidence to assess replication is of great assistance to interpretation. A development programme with two trials in which a critical subgroup finding can be assessed is clearly advantageous.
rules.source_section: Section 4.6 - Credibility of a subgroup finding
rules.verbatim_quote: Having two or more relevant sources of evidence, and hence being able to assess replication, is of great assistance to interpretation... a development programme with two trials in which a critical subgroup finding can be assessed is clearly advantageous.
rules.exceptions: A trial from outside the development programme, but with similar experimental conditions (similar intervention(s), variable(s) etc. with the relevant sub-population represented) might also be justified as providing relevant insight.
cross_references: CPMP/EWP/2330/99 - Points to consider on application with one pivotal study

rules.rule_text: The analysis of subgroups would usually proceed with an adjustment for those factors that were also included to stratify randomisation in line with the primary analysis.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1 - A strategy for the assessment of clinical trials
rules.verbatim_quote: The analysis of subgroups would usually proceed with an adjustment for those factors that were also included to stratify randomisation in line with the primary analysis.

rules.rule_text: The assessor should expect to find in the trial report a discussion of subgroup investigations conducted and their categorisation, indicating any differences from the plan outlined in the study protocol or analysis plan.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1 - A strategy for the assessment of clinical trials
rules.verbatim_quote: The assessor should expect to find in the trial report a discussion of subgroup investigations conducted and their categorisation, indicating any differences from the plan outlined in the study protocol or analysis plan.

rules.rule_text: If factors are mentioned as standard subgroup analyses in EMA guidelines or scientific literature they should be prioritised for evaluation, and at least included amongst the exploratory analyses.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1 - A strategy for the assessment of clinical trials
rules.verbatim_quote: If factors are mentioned as standard subgroup analyses in EMA guidelines or scientific literature they should be prioritised for evaluation, and at least included amongst the exploratory analyses.

rules.rule_text: For Assessment Scenario 1 (overall statistically persuasive efficacy), exploration of consistency of treatment effects should include subgroup analyses per Section 4.5 methods. If key subgroup assessment has been well planned and no inconsistent findings are apparent, investigation may be regarded as complete.
rules.source_section: Section 5.2 - Assessment scenario 1
rules.verbatim_quote: Exploration of consistency of treatment effects should include subgroup analyses in line with the methods outlined in Section 4.5. If the assessment of key subgroups has been well planned, nothing has arisen during the course of the trial to change the scientific assessment of plausibility and no evidence of inconsistent findings is apparent, then investigation may be regarded as being complete.
rules.exceptions: Inconsistent or extreme data in other exploratory subgroups, where absence of a plausible link to treatment response can be confirmed, could generally be disregarded unless replicated across more than one trial.

rules.rule_text: Inconsistency evidence in a subgroup is credible, and hence subject to further sponsor evaluation and regulatory consideration, if there is either (a) biological plausibility for an inconsistent effect in the direction expected, especially if replicated across multiple data sources, or (b) replication of the inconsistent finding across multiple data sources, especially if there is also biological plausibility.
rules.source_section: Section 5.2 - Assessment scenario 1
rules.verbatim_quote: If some evidence of inconsistency is observed for the effect in a subgroup (compared to the whole trial population) it may be considered credible, and hence subject to further sponsor evaluation and regulatory consideration, if there is either: a. biological plausibility for an inconsistent effect in the direction expected... b. replication of the inconsistent finding across multiple data sources.
rules.exceptions: In submissions with only one trial in which the subgroup can be properly assessed, the fact that there is no opportunity to find replication within the phase III clinical program cannot be taken as an argument to ignore the signal.

rules.rule_text: For Assessment Scenario 2 (borderline or unconvincing risk-benefit), for a subgroup to be considered credible, external evidence should exist that the subgroup is a well-defined and clinically relevant entity, and the subgroup should usually have been considered during trial planning (e.g. stratified randomisation or mentioned amongst key subgroups).
rules.source_section: Section 5.3 - Assessment scenario 2
rules.verbatim_quote: External evidence should exist that the subgroup of interest is a well-defined and clinically relevant entity. Usually it would be expected that the respective subgroup has been considered when planning the trial (e.g. stratified randomisation or that it has been mentioned amongst the key subgroups) with an argument provided as to why it is a clinically relevant entity.

rules.rule_text: For Assessment Scenario 2, a pharmacological rationale or mechanistically plausible explanation should exist as to why the drug under investigation could have different efficacy (or risk-benefit) in a sub-population and its complement (after considering the scale of assessment).
rules.source_section: Section 5.3 - Assessment scenario 2
rules.verbatim_quote: A pharmacological rationale, or a mechanistically plausible explanation, should exist, why the drug under investigation could have different efficacy (or risk-benefit) in a sub-population and its complement (after considering also the scale of assessment).

rules.rule_text: For Assessment Scenario 2, the totality of statistical evidence based on individual trials and pooled analyses should meet the same standards of evidence as would usually be expected for the all-randomised population, indicating that the size of the treatment effect in the subgroup is substantial as compared to the variability of the problem.
rules.source_section: Section 5.3 - Assessment scenario 2
rules.verbatim_quote: The totality of statistical evidence, based on individual trials and pooled analyses, should meet the same standards of evidence as would usually be expected for the all-randomised population indicating that the size of the treatment effect in the subgroup is substantial as compared to the variability of the problem.

rules.rule_text: Whenever a treatment recommendation is based on a subgroup, it is mandated that risk-benefit should be carefully inspected in that subgroup and the relevance of safety data from the all-randomised population to the subgroup must be carefully considered. Rare serious adverse events might, by chance alone, not occur in the subgroup of interest but should be considered in the risk-benefit assessment unless justified otherwise.
rules.source_section: Section 5.3 - Assessment scenario 2
rules.verbatim_quote: Whenever a treatment recommendation is to be based on a subgroup, it is mandated that risk-benefit should be carefully inspected in that subgroup and the relevance of safety data from the all-randomised population to the subgroup is carefully considered. Rare serious adverse events might, by chance alone, not occur in the subgroup of interest in the clinical trial, but should be considered in the risk-benefit assessment unless justified otherwise.

rules.rule_text: A close inspection of the baseline profiles of the subgroup is required regarding the comparison between treatment groups. Adjustment for differences might be required to ensure that an estimated treatment effect is not introduced or enhanced by baseline imbalances between groups.
rules.source_section: Section 5.3 - Assessment scenario 2
rules.verbatim_quote: A close inspection of the baseline profiles of the subgroup is required regarding the comparison between treatment groups. Adjustment for differences might be required to ensure that an estimated treatment effect is not introduced or enhanced by baseline imbalances between groups.

rules.rule_text: For Assessment Scenario 3 (failed primary endpoint), where a positive licensing decision is exceptionally considered, Section 5.3 criteria represent the minimum that should be fulfilled, plus a clear rationale must exist as to why a properly planned trial has failed despite the drug being regarded as efficacious and why additional prospective studies are unfeasible or unwarranted.
rules.source_section: Section 5.4 - Assessment scenario 3
rules.verbatim_quote: If nevertheless a positive licensing decision is, exceptionally, considered in this circumstance then Section 5.3 represents the minimum criteria that should be fulfilled. In addition, in such a situation, a clear rationale must exist as to why a properly planned trial has failed despite the drug being regarded as efficacious and why additional prospective studies to establish formal proof of efficacy are unfeasible or unwarranted.
rules.exceptions: Rare instances where trials are not feasible to repeat or situations where trials are of considerable size (e.g. cardiovascular diseases) and subpopulations bear considerable amounts of randomised evidence.

rules.rule_text: A strategy that assumes consistency of a population in terms of its likely response to treatment, without discussion and without planning and conducting further investigation, is not sufficient.
rules.source_section: Section 6.2 - A strategy for selection and definition of subgroups for assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: A strategy that assumes consistency of a population in terms of its likely response to treatment, without discussion and without planning and conducting further investigation, is not sufficient.

rules.rule_text: It is recommended that two levels of investigation be routinely considered: (1) 'key subgroups' including factors used in stratification of randomisation and other factors with biological plausibility for differential response; and (2) 'exploratory subgroups' across the spectrum of demographic, disease and clinical characteristics. A listing and categorisation of planned subgroup analyses should be presented in the trial protocol.
rules.source_section: Section 6.2 - A strategy for selection and definition of subgroups for assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: It is recommended that two levels of investigation should routinely be considered. The first level would include investigation of 'key subgroups'... Second, analyses of truly 'exploratory subgroups' should be planned for the spectrum of demographic, disease and clinical characteristics... A listing and categorisation of planned subgroup analyses should be presented in the trial protocol.

rules.rule_text: Restrictions of a trial population to a sub-population of the target population should be justified, detailing whether restrictions are made due to safety concerns, anticipated lack of efficacy, or other operational considerations.
rules.source_section: Section 6.1 - Considering heterogeneity within a target population
rules.verbatim_quote: Restrictions of a trial population to a sub-population of the target population should be justified, detailing whether restrictions are made due to safety concerns, anticipated lack of efficacy, or other operational considerations.

rules.rule_text: For multi-regional trials, it is recommended to include country or region as a factor into the randomisation model and the analysis, because country/region can be important prognostic factors covering important intrinsic and extrinsic factors.
rules.source_section: Section 6.2 - A strategy for selection and definition of subgroups for assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: With multi-regional trials it is recommended to include country or region as a factor into the randomisation model and the analysis (PtC on adjustment for baseline covariates), because including centre often becomes impractical as few patients are recruited per centre, across a large number of centres.
rules.exceptions: Including centre may become impractical as few patients are recruited per centre across a large number of centres.

rules.rule_text: The assessment report should reflect the discussion provided by the applicant about heterogeneity in the target population and potential sources for inconsistency in treatment effects. Any inconsistency should be discussed in respect of credibility and, if found credible, in terms of clinical relevance, benefit-risk and communication through the EPAR or SmPC. If inconsistent results are found not to be credible, this should be explicitly stated in the assessment report.
rules.source_section: Section 7 - Including results from subgroup analyses in assessment reports and product information
rules.verbatim_quote: Any inconsistency should be discussed, in respect of credibility and, if found to be credible, in terms of clinical relevance, benefit-risk and communication through the Public Assessment Report or SmPC. If inconsistent results are found not be credible, this should be explicitly stated in the assessment report.
cross_references: Guideline on Summary of Product Characteristics, Revision 2, September 2009

rules.rule_text: Where a subgroup finding found to be credible indicates that therapeutic efficacy or positive risk-benefit is not established, or that risk-benefit is negative, consequent labelling will be reflected in Section 4.1 or 4.3 of the SmPC. Where important uncertainty exists in a key subgroup despite overall positive findings, this can be expressed in a warning in Section 4.4 of the SmPC.
rules.source_section: Section 7 - Including results from subgroup analyses in assessment reports and product information
rules.verbatim_quote: Where a subgroup finding that is found to be credible indicates that therapeutic efficacy or positive risk-benefit is not established, or indeed that risk-benefit is negative, consequent labelling will be reflected in Section 4.1 or 4.3 of the SmPC, as appropriate.
cross_references: Guideline on Summary of Product Characteristics, Revision 2, September 2009

rules.rule_text: The exploration of interactions and effects in subgroups should proceed first on the scale on which the endpoint is commonly analysed. Where consistency turns out to be scale-dependent, irrespective of whether unexplained inconsistency remains or whether consistency is only seen on the relative scale, benefit/risk in subgroups needs to be assessed carefully when the risk profile of the population is not homogeneous.
rules.source_section: Section 4.5 - Methods for assessment of the consistency of the treatment effect in subgroups and associated data presentations
rules.verbatim_quote: It is recommended that the exploration of interactions and effects in subgroups proceeds first on the scale on which the endpoint is commonly analysed... Irrespective of whether unexplained inconsistency remains, or where consistency is only seen in the relative scale, benefit/risk in subgroups needs to be assessed carefully as soon as the risk profile of the population is not homogeneous.
rules.exceptions: It might be useful to present supplementary analyses on the complementary scale for those covariates or subgroups where inconsistency is observed.
